But investors overseas services was just a preposterous story But there was a reason as There always must be why gullibility could be created among people who should have known better as There was in Bernie's case in Bernie's case made off not cornfield The reason was what what? Who he was yeah, he'd been on The board of the New York Stock Exchange Well, you're gonna question him are you right so there's always one thing if you go look at these other two cases It's a different thing But there's always one thing my favorite gullibility ad is on 77 So this ran I've shrunken it in size This is a full-page ad From the tabloids it ran for almost eight years Well, it's legally compliant. It's very careful And it sells a little plastic Buddha that you rub its tummy and You get money Now you might expect to find it in the tabloids And that's of course mostly where it ran one of the funniest things about the ad to me We see if I find oh at the bottom of the coupon you can check off and you can get two Okay, so I'm not exactly sure why you would want to But but you can get to well, maybe a guy rubbed to maybe you get double the money, that's right All right, so now you might not think this has much in common with say the Bernie Madoff scandal But it has everything in common with the Bernie Madoff scandal All the way to the point that really what they're doing is rubbing Bernie's tummy and they're getting money. I mean It's just as silly on the surface. So Here's what they all have in common. Oh what I want to tell you the rest of the story first so You immediately think well, of course it ran in the tabloids Because everybody who reads the Daily Star and the National Enquirer, you know all of that You know is a moron right just like you don't buy lottery tickets. I'm sure you don't read that stuff, right? Right, right So you're not surprised that this ran in the National Enquirer It also ran on the back pages of daily newspapers across the country So we would argue Especially going back 25 years that that's a step up wouldn't we? It would be no surprise to you that the Wall Street Journal would refuse to run this ad They're pretty touchy But on a bet the guy ran it in investors business daily Which if you don't know investors business daily is basically a microcosm of the Wall Street Journal circulation They're almost all dupes They subscribe to both They don't get enough Boring unpleasant depressing financial information from one they want to So on a bet he ran it in investors business daily All right only ran it once because Powers that be an investor's business daily realized what they had just run and of course we're getting phone calls And I imagine the publisher was getting laughed at at the club and so forth So they wouldn't run it again even if he wanted to run it and it was the least profitable place he ran it So there was no strong motivation to run it continuously, but notice. I said the least profitable I didn't say loser They covered ad cost with a slight profit in investors business daily With rub the Buddha for money In investors business daily rub the Buddha for money a No man ever went broke over estimating the incredible ignorance of any segment of the American public but Page so the thing that hit that they all have in common The rub the Buddha for money and Bernie made off. This is very important. We'll come back to it later in the day It's really an aside now, but it's important. So what do they all have in common? What did home stake oil have in common what does rub the Buddha for money have in common what is Bernie made off have in common What did Bernie corn fell have in common? Here's what they all have in common the placement of faith in someone else or In the case of the little Buddha in something else not in self It's worth writing down exactly the way I just said it for those of you who are taking notes It's the placement of faith in someone else Parenthesis something else not in self Every achievement of gullibility with people who should know better has that has a foundational element it invites people to place their faith outside of self Why does it work? Because we talked earlier if you recall that even successful people tend to credit sources outside of self Even for their own success They tend to diminish their own role in fact Those of you in the room with profound religious background are probably Uncomfortable with the phrase self-made man It's a antithetical concept And deeply religious people will quick quickly deny it if they are introduced that way they will quickly deny it So even successful people and most people have been taught and ingrained to look outside of self so the scams all rely on it To achieve gullibility. It's almost impossible to achieve gullibility if the premise you're selling is all about faith in self
